title: Ling fangyi
creator: Unknown
file: Ritual wine container (fangyi) with masks (taotie), serpents, and birds.jpg
description: Ritual wine container (fangyi) with masks (taotie), serpents, and birds. White bronze patinated with azurite (chiefly inside), malachite and cuprite. Two inscriptions in ancient seal script. 35.3 x 24.8 x 23.3 cm This vessel is most famous for its lengthy cast inscription of 187 characters made by Zuoce Ling 作冊令 (“Recorder Ling”). The text, one of the longest from the early Zhou period, is repeated inside the vessel and the lid. The vessel commemorates three days of administrative meetings and ritual ceremonies held in Chengzhou during the reign of Zh…

title: Yi of Marquis of Qi
creator: Unknown
file: 20090822 Shanghai Museum 3256.jpg
description: Yi of Marquis Qi . A Yi is an ancient ritual bronze vessel from China. It is believed that it was used to hold water for washing the hands before rituals like sacrifices. This Yi has the shape of half a gourd with a handle in the shape of a dragon, and an oval base. It is supported by four legs decorated with taotie (animal mask designs). The bottom shows 22 chinese characters written in 4 lines, stating that "the Marquis of Qi cast this Yi for his wife Mengji Liangnii, the princess of the State of Guo". It is from the late Western Zhou Dynasty (-1046…

description: Dragon-Shaped Gong. Shang Dynasty, 16th - 11th century BC. Shanxi Museum This Shang dynasty gong is shaped, as usual, like a dragon with bottle horns; its body, however, is elongated compared to other examples. The object is a wine containter, made beautiful by the bronze-caster's art. The top of the dragons's body is a lid, that is lifted by the raised knob that can be seen in the photo. file: “洹子孟姜”青铜壶03666.jpg
description: "Huanzi Mengjiang" Bronze Hu (wine vessel). Spring and Autumn period, Qi state. The inscription inside the neck of this hu records that someone in the family of Huanzi Mengjiang (daughter of Qi Hou) died and Qi Hou wanted to be in mourning. This didnot conform to the standard rites. Qi Hou made a special request to the king through the administrator of rites and was permitted.

creator: Unknown
file: 楚屈子赤角簠05078.jpg
description: ​春秋 1976年随州鲢鱼嘴出土。盖、器内铭文相同：“佳正月初吉丁亥，楚屈子赤角媵仲芈璜飤簠，其眉寿无疆子子孙孙永保用之。”仅存之屈氏礼器。赤角或即《左传》中的息公子朱。

title: 春秋时代考叔□父铜簠
creator: Unknown
file: 考叔（左爿右言）父簠05082.jpg
description: ​春秋 1969年枝江百里洲镇八亩摊村王家岗。盖、器内铭文相同：“唯正月初吉丁亥，考叔（左爿右言）父自作尊簠，其眉寿万年无疆，子子孙孙永宝用之。”有研究认为此“考叔（左爿右言）父”即“塞公孙（左爿右言）父”。“塞”或为息国之息。

title: Chinese character forms of Han and Zi
creator: Unknown
file: Chinese character forms of Han and Zi.svg
description: A three-column SVG comparison of the Chinese characters "Han" (漢/汉) and "Zi" (字), read vertically from left to right, illustrating their evolution through ancient script, traditional calligraphy, and simplified forms. Left (Ancient / Bronze Inscriptions): "Han" is sourced from the bronze vessel Jing Shi Jun Zun (京師畯尊, Early Western Zhou dynasty); "Zi" is sourced from the bronze vessel Zi Fu Ji Zhi (字父己觶, Late Shang dynasty). Middle (Traditional): "Han" (漢) is taken from the Inscription on the Sweet Spring at Jiucheng Palace (九成宮醴泉銘, 632 AD) calligraph…

title: Bronze zhou jie with gold inlay
creator: Unknown
file: CMOC Treasures of Ancient China exhibit - bronze zhou jie, detail.jpg
description: Bronze zhou jie with gold inlay. Warring States Period (475 - 221 B.C.) Excavated at Shou County, Anhui Province, 1957 This shou jie (shipping pass) was issued by the King of Chu to Prince Qi, Lord of E. Prefecture. Such permits, reserved for royalty and aristocrats, specified the kind and quantity of goods to be transported; they also stated tax exemptions and tariffs. Verification of its authenticity consisted of the perfectly fitted halves of a section of bamboo.

description: Bronze zhou jie with gold inlay. Warring States Period (475 - 221 B.C.) Excavated at Shou County, Anhui Province, 1957 This zhou jie (舟节, shipping pass) was issued by the King of Chu to Prince Qi, Lord of E. Prefecture. Such permits, reserved for royalty and aristocrats, specified the kind and quantity of goods to be transported; they also stated tax exemptions and tariffs. Verification of its authenticity consisted of the perfectly fitted halves of a section of bamboo.

title: BLW Celadon dish with unglazed fish
creator: Unknown
file: BLW Celadon dish with unglazed fish.jpg
description: Celadon dish with unglazed fish Southern China, Longquan kilns Yuan dynasty, 1300-1400 Green-glazed stonewares from Zhejiang were the most common type of Chinese ceramics exported to the Middle East before 1400. This dish was thrown and carved before being given a thick green \'celadon\' glaze, which has pooled in the incised decoration and carved fluting. The four fish were made separately in moulds and applied to the unfired glaze. They turned red when the dish was fired. A chip on the rim reveals the contrast between the smooth smooth glaze and the…

title: beef noodle soup · II
creator: Unknown
file: Kuaitiao nuea pueay.JPG
description: Kuaitiao nuea pueay (Thai script: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเนื้อเปื่อย) is a noodle soup with tender braised beef. It is the Thai version of the Chinese "braised beef noodles" (Chinese script: (紅燒牛肉麵). This particular version is with sen yai noodles, also known as shahe fen in Chinese. Condiments for this dish are roasted dried chilli flakes, and a dip made with mashed green bird's eye chillies, ginger, garlic, sugar and vinegar.

title: Red lacquerware dish, Ming Dynasty
creator: Unknown
file: Red lacquerware dish, Ming Dynasty.jpg
description: A Chinese dish made of red lacquer over wood, from the Ming Dynasty, dated late 15th to mid 16th century. In the center design of this dish, two archaistic carved dragons without scales twist their bodies in reach of an endless knot, a symbol of longevity in Chinese culture. To make this dish even more auspicious, on the reverse side there is a carved design of chrysanthemums, flowers which are representative of long life in Chinese culture. The designs of billowing waves and lotus flowers carved into the background represent a watery environment, whi…

title: Cloisonné incense burners
creator: Unknown
file: Cloisonné incense burners.jpg
description: These extravagant perfumers, commissioned by the Qianlong emperor, reference the ritual tripods of the Shang and Zhou dynasties but use a palette of Qing colours. Both feature traditional Chinese emblems associated with long life, such as deer. Delicate-looking cranes, symbols of immortality, support the heavy burners, which would originally have graced an official room within the palace alongside other cloisonné items. Oing dynasty Qianlong period, 1736-95 Beijing, China Donated by Sir H.S.E. Vanderpant. Picture taken at the British Museum, London, UK.

title: Decorative Object, Qing period · VIII
creator: Unknown
file: Silk Roads Beyond Borders exhibition, Poly MGM Museum, January 21, 2026 82.jpg
description: in the Poly MGM Museum, Macau Large Cloisonné Enamel Ram and Vase Group Height 56cm This artifact takes the form of a ram bearing a precious zun-vase on its back. It is covered overall in a sky-blue enamel glaze, with patterns executed in vibrant red, white, and deep blue glazes. Its horns, ears, legs, as well as its long beard, back hair, and tail hair, are all adorned with finely gilded copper. This exquisite work is a masterpiece of cloisonné craftsmanship from the Qianlong period.

title: Pair of lidded meiping (梅瓶) vases - Reign of the Qianlong emperor - China - Louvre - TH 302 and
creator: Unknown
file: Pair of lidded meiping (梅瓶) vases - Reign of the Qianlong emperor (1736-1795) - China - Louvre - TH 302 and 303.jpg
description: Paire de vases meiping couverts - Règne de Qianlong (1736-1795) - Chine - Bronze - émail cloisonné - Louvre TH 302 et 303. Le site du Louvre indique : À l’origine récipient à vin, le vase meiping( 梅瓶)servit à recevoir des branches de prunier (mei) fleuries à partir des Song, d’où son nom. Sa haute épaule renflée, son goulot cylindrique souvent étroit, parfois large, et sa base resserrée ou cintrée en font une pièce immédiatement reconnaissable. Ici le décor en émail cloisonné est très riche: demi-feuilles lancéolées à la base surmontées par plusieurs …

title: Vase with Three Rams' Heads
creator: Unknown
file: China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen, Qing dynasty (1644-1911), Qianlong mar - Vase with Three Rams' Heads - 1989.321 - Cleveland Museum of Art.tif
description: In cloisonné enamelling, the decorative patterns are outlined by soldering metal wires (usually copper) to a metal base, creating a network of cells, or cloisons, to be filled with powdered enamels. After firing, the enamels are polished to create a smooth surface level with the cloisons. The exposed metal surfaces are gilt.

description: Cat.105. Chinese. Imperial Throne. Qing dynasty, Qianlong reign, 1736–95. Carved polychrome lacquer on wood core, 47 x 49½ x 36" (119.3 x 125.7 x 91.4 cm). V&A W399–1922 Artefacts from the Chinese imperial court, including this grand and ornate throne (and cats. 106–107), enthralled many nineteenth-century collectors and consequently figure prominently in the Museum’s collection. title: Jade Cong from China, Neolithic Period
creator: Unknown
file: Jade Cong from China, Neolithic Period.jpg
description: "Among Neolithic jades, the cong displays the most complex form: a cylindrical tube encased in a square prism that gently tapers from top to bottom. Simple masks with circular eyes and bar-shaped mouths decorate the corners. Enigmatic in function and meaning, the cong probably signified wealth and social status. In a tomb found at Sidun, Jiangsu Province, numerous cong lay in a circle around the dead, suggesting that they also served a religious or ritual purpose." (Metropolitan Museum of Art object description)

title: Jade disc (Bi), a flat circular slab of jade with a central hole
creator: Unknown
file: Jade disc (Bi). Nephrite jade. Liangzhu Culture, south-east China. Neolithic period, c. 2500 BCE. Victoria and Albert Museum, London.jpg
description: Jade disc (Bi), a flat circular slab of jade with a central hole. An ancient Chinese text entitled the "Zhouli" (Rituals of Zhou) describes the "bi" as a ceremonial object to sacrifice to Heaven. The precise significance is unknown; jade discs were found in many graves and might have had a ritual role. Nephrite jade, variegated dark green and brown. Liangzhu Culture, south-east China. Neolithic period, c. 2500 BCE. Victoria and Albert Museum, London.

title: Coupe ambassade de Siam Exposition Jade 30122016
creator: Unknown
file: Coupe ambassade de Siam Exposition Jade 30122016 1.jpg
description: The cup known as the Siamese Embassy Cup of 1686 (perhaps the cup presented to Louis XIV on that occasion). Jade, Ming dynasty. This cup was part of Balthazar Sage's collection and is now in the collection of the National Museum of Natural History in Paris. It was featured in the temporary exhibition "Jade, from Emperors to Art Deco" at the Guimet Museum, from October 19, 2016 to January 16, 2017.

title: Tasse et présentoir Dynastie Qing Fontainebleau 30122016
creator: Unknown
file: Tasse et présentoir Dynastie Qing Fontainebleau 30122016.jpg
description: Tasse avec couvercle en jade cerclé d'or, sur un présentoir en or. Offert par l'ambassade de l'empereur de Siam à Napoléon III en 1861. Chine, dynastie Qing ( 1644-1911 ), 18ème ou 19ème siècle. Sous cette dynasie, l'usage de bol en jade sur un présentoir en or était réservé en Chine à l'empereur et à l'impératrice. Conservé au Musée National du Château de Fontainebleau ( Références F 1692 C1 et C2 ). Présenté dans l'exposition temporaire " Jade, des empereurs à l'Art Déco " au Musée Guimet, du 19 Octobre 2016 au 16 Janvier 2017.

title: The Daoist Immortal Han Xiangzi
creator: Liu Jun
file: Han Xiangzi, by Ming court painter Liu Jun.jpg
description: This painting depicts the Daoist immortal Han Xiangzi (韓湘子) miraculously treading the surface of a turbulent sea and playing a tune with a bamboo flute. The immortal wears a rustic cape of sawtoothed leaves and curly vegetal tendrils, pants with a skirt of reeds, a satchel lined with animal fur over his shoulder, and a belt holding two dried gourds.

title: Zhong Kui
creator: Ren Bonian
file: 清 任頤 鍾馗 軸-Zhong Kui MET 2002 208 2 O.jpg
description: "Ren Yi, a leader of the late Qing Shanghai school of painting, was a disciple of the painter Ren Xiong (1820–1857). A prolific artist, Ren Yi commanded a wide range of techniques. In this depiction of Zhong Kui, the legendary Demon Queller, for example, he adopted the figural style of Chen Hongshou (1598–1652). During the late Qing dynasty, Zhong Kui became a popular symbol of Chinese opposition to the misrule of the Manchus and was often portrayed glowering ferociously over a motley retinue of cowering ghosts and demons. In Ren Yi's depiction, howev…

title: Luoyang
creator: Unknown
description: Luoyang is an industrial city in central China’s Henan province, in what is widely recognized as the region where Chinese civilization originated. Luoyang, ancient China’s capital during multiple dynasties, is home to Baima Si (White Horse Temple), among China’s first Buddhist temples, founded in the 1st century.

title: Mawangdui painted silk funeral banner
creator: Unknown

description: Funeral banner, painting on silk, from the tomb #1 of Mawangdui, Western Han period. Hunan Provincial Museum, Changsha, China. 2.05 x 0.92 m. This 6¾ foot long Western Han painting on silk was found draped over the coffin in the grave of Lady Dai (c. 168 BC) at Mawangdui near Changsha in Hunan province, China. The scenes depicted on it seem to illustrate the journey of the woman's soul. The top section shows the heavenly realm, complete with dragons, leopards, and hybrid creatures. At the corners are the crow that symbolizes the sun and the toad that …

title: Western Han Chinese Silk
creator: Unknown
file: Western Han Chinese Silk.JPG
description: This 6¾ foot long Western Han painting on silk was found draped over the coffin in the grave of Lady Dai (c. 168 BC) at Mawangdui near Changsha in Hunan province, China. The scenes depicted on it seem to illustrate the journey of the woman's soul. The top section shows the heavenly realm, complete with dragons, leopards, and hybrid creatures. At the corners are the crow that symbolizes the sun and the toad that symbolizes the moon, the pairing of the sun and moon representing the cosmic forces of yin and yang.

title: Tigers Chasing Deer, with Dragon
creator: Unknown
file: China, Eastern, 11th-12th century - Tigers Chasing Deer, with Dragon - 1988.100 - Cleveland Museum of Art.tif
description: The design of this Central Asian tapestry is a synthesis of Western and Chinese elements. Bands of pearls and split palmettes and the deer with mushroom-shaped antlers originated in Iran and Sogdiana but migrated to China during the Tang dynasty (618-907). Although these motifs disappeared from Chinese art after the fall of the Tang, they survived in Central Asia for centuries. The dragon is Chinese in origin, but has a Central Asian form (particularly its extended snout). Also characteristic of Central Asia are the placement of the dragon among flowe…

title: Traveling on the River in Snow
creator: Unknown
file: Guo Zhongshu-Traveling on the River in Snow.jpg
description: Traveling on the River in Clearing Snow , by Guo Zhongshu (c. 910–977 AD). This painting shows a pair of Chinese cargo ships (with stern-mounted rudders) accompanied by a smaller craft. It is painted on silk and dated to the early Song Dynasty period (960–1279 AD).

title: daoist robe
creator: Unknown
file: Daoist Priest's Robe (China), 19th century (CH 18464275).jpg
description: Robe in the form of a large rectangle composed of widths of fabric which are continuous from the front hem over the shoulder to the back hem. The garment is open down the center front, and the front borders are continuous with the neck band. The borders on the outside edges are also continuous over the arm, and have openings for the hands. Each front panel contains two medallions with a full-face dragon chasing a magic pearl in the sky above a mountain. On the back, in the upper center is a large medallion with the "sacred Pagoda" (Lamaist) plus two s…

title: Military Official's Rank Badge
creator: Unknown
file: Military Official's Rank Badge.jpg
description: During the Ming dynasty, imperial officials wore colorful badges to identify their rank at formal ceremonies. Badges with ferocious animals delineated military officers and badges with birds denoted civil representatives. This is a military rank badge depicting a striding lion.

title: five-clawed dragon
creator: Unknown
file: Kesi roundel with five-clawed dragon design.jpg
credit: Hali photographer? Or museum? Artwork by unknown Chinese weaver
description: Kesi roundel with five-clawed dragon design, China, late 17th century. Silk, metallic thread and peacock feather slit-tapestry weave, 0.30 x 0.31 m (11¾” x 12½”). Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York, Fletcher Fund, 36.65.32 Native to the Indian subcontinent, the peacock (Pavo cristatus) has long been prized in South Asia and further afield for its brilliant blue-green colouring and showy ocelli tail feathers—around 200 of which make up a mature bird’s train. These are moulted and regrown annually. Occasionally, individual peacock feather barbules wer…

description: Robe, dragon, man's; plum-red silk, coiled dragon motif, embroidered in silk and gold thread; unusual Manchu hoof cuffs and very narrow cut; appears to have been shortened and narrowed; unlined mainly in blues and pinks and gold thread; plain black sleeves, no braiding; Pekin stitch; braid; embroidered in gold thread and multicoloured silk embroidery; gold and black striped sleeves, no collar. Note- a ch'ao-fu is a formal garment consisting of two parts, an upper hip length riding style coat, and a lower paired overlapping skirt. It was worn only for …

title: Each warrior had a different face
creator: Unknown
file: Each warrior had a different face (35687732155).jpg
credit: shankar s. from Dubai, united arab emirates
description: Every figure in the Terracotta warriors army differs in facial features and expression, clothing, hairstyle, and gestures, providing abundant and detailed artifacts for the study of the military, cultural, and economic history of that period. Some skeptics were wondering if the king had killed off his army just before his death which resulted the face of each individual to be copied. Closer scrutiny has however shown that there are basically ten kinds of faces, which were used round and round and in an assorted fashion. More on this in a later caption…

title: Lush greenery around the Terracotta warriors pit
creator: Unknown
file: Lush greenery around the Terracotta warriors pit (35648438506).jpg
credit: shankar s. from Dubai, united arab emirates
description: Tickets in hand, meeting one of the farmers who discovered the buried terracotta figures and gift purchases over, it was now time to move towards the excavation site. It's a short walk, and by this time it had started drizzling pretty heavily. There are three excavation pits in all, of which we will be exploring one. (I think it was Pit # 1). Here we are, my guide Alena and I, walking towards the Pit # 1. Some lush greenery here. (Xi'an, Shaanxi, China, May 2017)
